topic: Philosophy of Religion: Religious Experience, Problem of Evil
subject: religious-studies

items.q: Does explaining an experience's brain mechanism automatically debunk it?
items.correct: Not by itself - how an experience is produced and whether its object is real are distinct questions, though naturalistic explanations can weaken its evidential force.
items.misconception: Any experience with a physiological trigger is thereby proved delusional.
items.random_wrong: Neuroscience has shown that experiences cannot occur in the brain.
items.why: The cause/content distinction (and the genetic fallacy) is the pivot of the whole debate about experience as evidence.

items.q: Does the free will defence cover earthquakes and disease?
items.correct: Its direct force concerns moral evil; natural evil requires extra resources, such as Augustine's account of a fallen world or Hick's claim that a law-governed, risky world is needed for soul-making.
items.misconception: Free will on its own neatly explains all natural disasters.
items.random_wrong: The free will defence says humans choose for volcanoes to erupt.
items.why: Matching each response to the kind of evil it addresses is a basic but frequently missed analytical move.

items.q: Is Augustine's 'privation' claim a denial that suffering is real?
items.correct: No - privation means evil is an absence or corruption of good, like blindness as absence of sight; the suffering is real but evil is not a created substance.
items.misconception: Augustine said evil is an illusion nobody actually experiences.
items.random_wrong: Privation is the name of a demon in Augustine's writings.
items.why: Misreading privation as unreality makes Augustine's theodicy look absurd and prevents fair evaluation.

items.q: What are James's four marks of mystical experience?
items.correct: Ineffability, noetic quality, transiency and passivity
items.why: Correct answer: Ineffability, noetic quality, transiency and passivity. “Light, heat, sound and motion” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which is an example of a corporate religious experience?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The Toronto Blessing. “Paul's Damascus road conversion” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.correct: The claim that omnipotence, perfect goodness and the existence of evil cannot all be true
items.random_wrong: The three persons of the Trinity
items.why: It's The claim that omnipotence, perfect goodness and the existence of evil cannot all be true. “Three proofs of God's existence” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What does Hick mean by calling the world a 'vale of soul-making'?
items.correct: A challenging environment enables free moral and spiritual growth towards God
items.misconception: The world is a perfect paradise
items.why: The answer examiners want is: A challenging environment enables free moral and spiritual growth towards God. “The world is a perfect paradise” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
